content:
# Revelation Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/REV_007_page_001.jpg (page 282), REV_007_page_002.jpg (page 283)

### The 144,000 Sealed and the Vast Multitude

1. After this I saw four angels standing at the four corners of the earth, holding back the four winds of the earth so that no wind would blow on the land or the sea or against any tree.

2. Then I saw another angel rising from the east, carrying the seal of the living God. He called out in a loud voice to the four angels who had been given power to harm the land and the sea:

3. "Do not harm the land or the sea or the trees until we have placed the seal of God on the foreheads of his servants!"

4. And I heard the number of those who were sealed: one hundred forty-four thousand, sealed from every tribe of the people of Israel:

5. Twelve thousand from the tribe of Judah were sealed, twelve thousand from the tribe of Reuben, twelve thousand from the tribe of Gad,

6. twelve thousand from Asher, twelve thousand from Naphtali, twelve thousand from Manasseh,

7. twelve thousand from Simeon, twelve thousand from Levi, twelve thousand from Issachar,

8. twelve thousand from Zebulun, twelve thousand from Joseph, twelve thousand from Benjamin.

9. After this I looked, and there before me was a vast crowd that no one could count -- people from every nation, tribe, people, and language. They stood before the throne and before the Lamb, wearing white robes and holding palm branches in their hands.

10. And they were shouting with a mighty voice: "Salvation belongs to our God, who sits on the throne, and to the Lamb!"

11. All the angels stood in a circle around the throne and around the elders and the four living creatures. They fell facedown before the throne and worshiped God,

12. saying: "Amen! Praise and glory and wisdom and thanksgiving and honor and power and strength belong to our God forever and ever! Amen!"

13. Then one of the elders asked me, "Who are these people dressed in white robes, and where have they come from?"

14. I answered, "Sir, you know the answer." And he told me, "These are the ones who have come through the time of terrible suffering. They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.

15. That is why they stand before God's throne and serve him day and night in his temple. And the one who sits on the throne will shelter them with his presence.

16. Never again will they be hungry. Never again will they be thirsty. The sun will not beat down on them, nor will any scorching heat.

17. For the Lamb at the center of the throne will be their shepherd. He will lead them to springs of living water. And God will wipe every tear from their eyes."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

Chapter 7 is a dramatic interlude between the sixth and seventh seals -- a breathtaking answer to the terrified question of 6:17: "Who is able to survive?" The answer: those sealed by God, and a multitude beyond counting.

**Verse 1 -- "four corners of the earth"** (ΤΕΣΣΑΡΑΣ ΓΩΝΙΑΣ ΤΗΣ ΓΗΣ): This is cosmic geography, not literal cartography. The image conveys totality -- God's angels controlling the forces of nature from every direction.

**Verse 4 -- "one hundred forty-four thousand"**: The Sinaiticus text gives the number as ΡΜΔ ΧΙΛΙΑΔΕΣ. Whether this figure is symbolic (12 tribes x 12,000 = perfect completeness) or literal has been debated for millennia. I present the number plainly and let the reader encounter it directly.

**Verse 9 -- "a vast crowd that no one could count"** (ΟΧΛΟΣ ΠΟΛΥΣ ΟΝ ΑΡΙΘΜΗΣΑΙ ΑΥΤΟΝ ΟΥΔΕΙΣ ΕΔΥΝΑΤΟ): The Greek emphasizes impossibility -- not merely "a great multitude" but a throng so enormous that counting it was beyond anyone's ability. "Vast crowd that no one could count" captures both the scale and the impossibility.

**Verse 14 -- "the time of terrible suffering"** (ΤΗΣ ΘΛΙΨΕΩΣ ΤΗΣ ΜΕΓΑΛΗΣ): Literally "the great tribulation." I chose "the time of terrible suffering" because "tribulation" has become a theological technical term that can distance readers from the raw human experience the Greek word thlipsis conveys -- pressure, crushing, anguish.

**Verse 14 -- "washed their robes... in the blood of the Lamb"**: This deliberately paradoxical image -- cleaning garments in blood -- is one of Revelation's most powerful metaphors. Blood that should stain instead purifies. I preserved this paradox without explanation, trusting its power.

**Verse 15 -- "shelter them with his presence"** (ΣΚΗΝΩΣΕΙ ΕΠ ΑΥΤΟΥΣ): Literally "will tent/tabernacle over them." This echoes God's shekinah presence in the wilderness tabernacle. "Shelter them with his presence" conveys both the protective covering and the intimate dwelling.

**Verses 16-17 -- the pastoral reversal**: The Lamb becomes a shepherd -- a stunning role reversal. Those who suffered hunger, thirst, and scorching heat will never experience them again. The chapter ends with one of Scripture's most tender images: God personally wiping tears from every eye.

-- PNEUMA
